howItWorks.intro: This tax penalty calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Filing Penalty = 5% per month x Unpaid Tax (max 25%)
Payment Penalty = 0.5% per month x Unpaid Tax (max 25%)
Interest = Unpaid Tax x (Federal Rate + 3%) / 12 x Months
Combined: Filing penalty reduced by payment penalty amount when both apply
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates. Consult a professional for critical decisions.

faq.question: What is the penalty for filing taxes late?
faq.answer: The late filing penalty is 5% of unpaid taxes per month, up to 25% maximum. If you file more than 60 days late, the minimum penalty is $485 or 100% of unpaid tax (whichever is less). Filing an extension avoids this penalty.

faq.question: What is the penalty for paying taxes late?
faq.answer: The late payment penalty is 0.5% of unpaid taxes per month, up to 25% maximum. Interest also accrues at the federal short-term rate plus 3% (currently about 8% annually).

faq.question: Can I avoid tax penalties?
faq.answer: File on time even if you cannot pay (to avoid the much larger filing penalty). Request an installment agreement or apply for penalty abatement if you have reasonable cause (illness, disaster, bad tax advice).

faq.question: Does filing an extension help with penalties?
faq.answer: An extension gives you 6 extra months to FILE without penalty, but it does NOT extend the deadline to PAY. You still owe interest and late payment penalties on any unpaid balance after April 15.

sections.title: What Is the Tax Penalty Calculator?
sections.body: The Tax Penalty Calculator helps you estimate your tax obligations accurately so you can plan ahead and avoid surprises at filing time. Instead of guessing or spending hours on manual calculations, get accurate results in seconds. Enter your details above and let the calculator do the work.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Tax obligations vary significantly based on your income, deductions, filing status, and jurisdiction. Small miscalculations compound over time and can lead to penalties or missed refund opportunities. Running your numbers through this calculator before filing season gives you a clear picture of where you stand and time to adjust your strategy.
